Daily HHS Accessibility Report — 2026-07-17#

Run ID: run-2026-07-17-986fd94e50 · Status: partial · Source data: 2026-07-15 · Generated: 2026-07-17T09:55:41.653Z

View HTML/CSS/JS Code Quality report for this date →

About These Reports#

U.S. federal government website traffic data is publicly available at analytics.usa.gov, powered by the Digital Analytics Program (DAP) — a shared analytics service that tracks traffic across hundreds of participating federal agencies and publishes the data openly. This report focuses on the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) and its sub-agencies, using HHS-specific traffic data from analytics.usa.gov/health-human-services.

This report measures the quality and accessibility of the top 100 most-visited HHS URLs as reported by analytics.usa.gov. Each day, Lighthouse scans are run against these URLs to measure:

  • Performance - How fast pages load for users (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Accessibility - How well pages work for users with disabilities, following WCAG guidelines (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Best Practices - Whether pages follow modern web development standards (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • SEO - How well pages are optimized for search engines (scores 0-100, higher is better)

Accessibility findings come from axe-core, the industry-standard accessibility testing engine embedded in Lighthouse. The axe findings surface specific WCAG violations such as missing alternative text, insufficient color contrast, and missing form labels that make government websites harder to use for people with disabilities.

Traffic data reflects daily visitor counts from analytics.usa.gov for HHS properties. URLs are ranked by page load count, ensuring the most-used HHS pages are prioritized for quality measurement.

Accessibility Trend Narrative#

Over the past 30 days of data (2026-06-17 to 2026-07-16), government website accessibility scores have improved (+1 points, from 92.71 to 93.71). Performance scores have declined (-1.03 points, from 53.89 to 52.86).

Today's aggregate accessibility score of 92.8 reflects the mean Lighthouse accessibility score across 98 successfully scanned government URLs. A score above 90 indicates generally strong compliance with WCAG automated checks, though manual testing is always recommended to fully assess accessibility.

Common Accessibility Issues (Top 10)#

The following axe-core rules were most frequently violated across scanned URLs today. These patterns indicate systemic accessibility barriers present across multiple government websites.

Human impact narratives for top issues

The following narratives describe the real-world impact of each accessibility barrier on people with disabilities. These barriers are not abstract technical failures; they prevent citizens from independently accessing government services.

target-size: Digital Motor Access

Small touch targets act as a digital gatekeeper, excluding individuals with tremors, arthritis, or limited dexterity from accessing essential services independently. These technical failures transform a routine interaction into a source of failure, stripping away the autonomy of citizens who require a frictionless, accessible interface to participate in digital life. The approximately 58 million Americans with ambulatory or self-care disabilities are disproportionately impacted by inadequate touch target sizing on government mobile websites.

Affected groups:

  • People with Parkinson's disease, arthritis, or hand tremors
  • Older adults with reduced fine motor control
  • People with motor disabilities using alternative pointing devices
  • People in situational impairment contexts (e.g., commuting, holding a child)

landmark-one-main: Primary Content Identification

Without a main landmark, screen reader users lose their most efficient way to skip to the primary content of a government page. Every page visit requires tabbing through the entire navigation structure to reach the actual content, a significant time and effort burden that accumulates across repeated visits to government websites for citizens managing ongoing benefit cases or legal matters.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

heading-order: Document Structure Navigation

Screen reader users navigate complex government websites primarily through heading structure, using headings as a table of contents to jump between sections. Skipped heading levels break the logical document outline, causing confusion about the hierarchy of information and forcing users to re-read sections to understand the relationship between topics, adding significant time and effort to information-gathering tasks.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

color-contrast: Visual Information Access

Low contrast text is one of the most pervasive barriers on government websites, rendering critical information invisible to the approximately 26 million Americans with low vision or color blindness. When agency announcements, form instructions, error messages, or legal notices are displayed in insufficient contrast, affected citizens are denied equal access to the information they need to exercise their rights and access public services.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision including age-related vision loss
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

link-in-text-block: Color-Independent Link Identification

When links are distinguished only by color, people with color vision deficiency cannot identify them as links and may miss critical references to additional information, forms, or resources. On government websites, overlooked links in informational content can mean missing essential instructions, deadlines, or procedures that affect a citizen's ability to access services or comply with requirements.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision who may not perceive color differences
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

link-name: Link Purpose Clarity

Links without accessible names are completely useless to screen reader users who navigate government pages by jumping between links. An unnamed link could lead anywhere, and activating it unknowingly could trigger downloads, open unexpected pages, or initiate unintended processes. On government websites, unnamed links undermine the informed consent principle by preventing citizens from knowing where a link will take them.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

tabindex: Focus Order Predictability

Positive tabindex values create unpredictable focus sequences that jump around the page in ways that differ from the visual reading order. For keyboard users and screen reader users on government websites, an erratic tab order makes it difficult to reliably navigate forms and interactive content, increasing errors and the time needed to complete tasks, and creating particular frustration for users with cognitive disabilities who need a predictable, logical interaction flow.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf using keyboard-only navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard or switch access

html-has-lang: Language Identification

Without a language attribute, screen readers default to the user's system language settings, which may cause government content to be read in the wrong language with incorrect pronunciation. This is particularly harmful for multilingual citizens accessing government services in their non-primary language, and for users of braille displays that depend on language metadata for proper rendering.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with cognitive or language differences

label: Form Field Identification

Unlabeled form fields are the most common barrier preventing blind and low-vision citizens from completing government forms. When input fields lack programmatic labels, screen reader users cannot determine what information is being requested, making it impossible to apply for benefits, file taxes, register to vote, or access virtually any government digital service that requires form input. This single failure can block an entire service interaction.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People with low vision relying on assistive technology
  • People who are deaf using keyboard navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using voice control

label-content-name-mismatch: Voice Control Activation

When the accessible name of an element does not match its visible text, voice control users cannot activate it using the words they see on screen. Saying "click Submit" to a button whose visual label is "Submit" but whose accessible name is "submitbtn" results in no action. This mismatch silently breaks voice control for citizens with motor disabilities who depend on this input method to interact with government websites.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People with low vision relying on screen magnifiers
  • People who are deaf using keyboard navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using voice control software
Disability icon key
Without Vision — People who are blind or have no functional vision (1.0% of U.S. population — ~3,400,000 Americans)
Limited Vision — People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast (2.4% of U.S. population — ~8,100,000 Americans)
Without Perception of Color — People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness) (4.3% of U.S. population — ~14,500,000 Americans)
Without Hearing — People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content (0.3% of U.S. population — ~1,100,000 Americans)
Limited Hearing — People with hearing loss who may struggle with audio without accommodations (3.5% of U.S. population — ~11,900,000 Americans)
Without Speech — People who cannot use speech or voice-based input effectively (0.5% of U.S. population — ~1,700,000 Americans)
Limited Manipulation — People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity (2.2% of U.S. population — ~7,600,000 Americans)
Limited Reach and Strength — People with limited reach, strength, or stamina (5.8% of U.S. population — ~19,600,000 Americans)
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities — People with cognitive, learning, or language differences (4.7% of U.S. population — ~15,900,000 Americans)

These icons show which groups of people with disabilities are excluded by each accessibility barrier. Where page view data is available, each icon shows an estimated number of people potentially excluded (page loads for affected URLs × disability prevalence rate from U.S. Census ACS 2023). Hover over or focus an icon to see the full estimate and methodology. Icons follow the Section 508 Functional Performance Criteria and the equivalent EU EN 301 549 v3.2.1 Table B.2 categories.

Prevalence data: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; supplemental data from CDC, NIDCD, AFB, and NIH/NEI. U.S. population base: ~336,500,000 (2023 estimate). Reviewed annually.

See the Section 508 WCAG to FPC mapping for additional detail on how accessibility requirements map to functional needs.

Usability Heuristics Summary#

The most common accessibility violations map to Nielsen Norman Group's 10 usability heuristics. This table shows which heuristics are most affected by today's top axe-core violations, helping to identify where systemic usability barriers exist.

Americans Excluded by Disability Today#

Based on 12,610,786 page loads across successfully scanned government URLs and U.S. disability prevalence rates from the U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101 (2023), an estimated 490,000 Americans encountered an accessibility barrier on a government website today.

Each row shows the number of people in a disability group who visited a page with at least one accessibility issue that affects their group. Prevalence rates and U.S. population estimates are derived from the American Community Survey (ACS) and supplemental sources. These figures are rough estimates intended to illustrate the scale of accessibility barriers.

Estimated Americans excluded today by disability category (2023 Census data)
Icon Disability Group (Section 508 FPC) U.S. Prevalence U.S. Population Affected Page Loads with Barriers Est. Americans Excluded Today
Limited Reach and Strength 5.8% ~19,600,000 3,050,000 170,000
Without Perception of Color 4.3% ~14,500,000 2,960,000 120,000
Limited Manipulation 2.2% ~7,600,000 4,080,000 89,771
Limited Vision 2.4% ~8,100,000 2,970,000 71,507
Without Vision 1.0% ~3,400,000 2,300,000 23,097
Without Hearing 0.3% ~1,100,000 2,300,000 6,929
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities 4.7% ~15,900,000 8,399 395

Sources: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; disability prevalence from ACS 2023 and supplemental CDC/NIDCD/NIH data. Reviewed annually.

Performance Impact on Americans#

Google defines a good Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) as under 2.5 seconds and recommends pages under 1.6 MB. The figures below estimate how much extra time Americans spend waiting, and how much extra data is transferred, because government websites fall short of these benchmarks. Calculations are based on 87 successfully scanned URLs with Lighthouse timing data.

Estimated performance impact vs. Google benchmarks for government URLs scanned on July 17, 2026
Metric Estimated total (July 17, 2026) Notes
Extra time waiting (vs 2.5s LCP benchmark) 9 months, 16 daysExtra time is calculated as: for each scanned URL, max(0, actual LCP − 2.5s) × page loads. Total: 25,061,444 seconds. 25,061,444 seconds
Extra data transferred (vs 1.6 MB page weight benchmark) ~5.8 TB (242 copies of Wikipedia)Extra data is calculated as: for each scanned URL, max(0, actual page weight − 1.6 MB) × page loads. Across 87 URLs with weight data

LCP and page weight are measured by Lighthouse. Wikipedia copy count uses a size of 24.05 GB per Wikipedia:Size of Wikipedia. These are rough estimates based on a sample of the top government URLs by traffic. Hover or focus the estimated totals for calculation details.

Content Density (Words per Megabyte)#

Words-per-Megabyte (WpM) measures how efficiently a page delivers readable content relative to its total download size. A low ratio indicates Digital Bloat—the page transfers far more data than the text it contains. Pages below 200 WpM are flagged for optimization review.

  • URLs with content metrics: 67
  • Average word count: 1,053
  • Average Words/MB: 885
  • URLs below 200 WpM threshold: 47
Content density for top URLs scanned on July 17, 2026 (up to 10 shown)

Word counts are extracted from the page’s main article content using Mozilla Readability, which strips navigation, sidebars, and ads. Page weight is the total resource size measured by Lighthouse. Pages below 200 WpM may impose unnecessary data costs on users on metered connections.

Detected Technologies#

Technology signals detected from the network requests loaded by each scanned URL. CMS detection identifies WordPress, Drupal, and Joomla from characteristic asset paths. USWDS detection identifies use of the U.S. Web Design System.

CMSURLsShare
Drupal12Sites: cdrh-rst.fda.gov, cms.gov, grants.nih.gov, hhs.gov, nhlbi.nih.gov, niaaa.nih.gov, niams.nih.gov, nichd.nih.gov, nida.nih.gov, nih.gov, nimh.nih.gov, ninds.nih.gov12%
WordPress6Sites: circulatingnow.nlm.nih.gov, mainweb.awsprod.nlm.nih.gov, ncbiinsights.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov, nlm.nih.gov, nlmdirector.nlm.nih.gov, sites.wip.nlm.nih.gov6%

USWDS detected on 43 of 98 scanned URLs (44%).

Observed USWDS versions: 2.4.0Sites: lhncbc.nlm.nih.gov, mor.nlm.nih.gov, 3.7.1Sites: classification.nlm.nih.gov. The latest release is available at github.com/uswds/uswds/releases.

Third-Party JavaScript Services#

Third-party scripts detected from network requests loaded by scanned pages. These services may affect page performance, user privacy, and security posture.

9 services marked ⚠ tracking send user data to third-party servers and may have privacy implications for site visitors.

Accessibility Statements (M-24-08)#

OMB Memorandum M-24-08 requires each federal agency to publish a digital accessibility statement that includes contact information for accessibility issues, known limitations, and a link to the agency Section 508 program page.

47 of 98 domains (48%) checked have a detectable accessibility statement at a standard URL path.

Required Federal Links (M-17-06)#

OMB Memorandum M-17-06 and the 21st Century IDEA Act require federal agency public websites to provide links to a Privacy Policy, Contact page, and FOIA page. These were among the compliance criteria benchmarked by the performance.gov federal website performance initiative (no longer active, but the underlying requirements remain in force).

39 of 98 domains (40%) have all three required link types detectable at standard URL paths.

Section 508: Legal Requirements vs. Best Practices#

Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act was enacted in 1998 and requires U.S. federal agencies to make their electronic and information technology accessible to people with disabilities. The technical standards were significantly updated through the ICT Accessibility Standards refresh, which became effective in January 2018 — the same year Apple celebrated the 10th anniversary of the iPhone. That refresh incorporated WCAG 2.0 Level AA (published in 2008) as the baseline for web content.

The web has changed dramatically since then. What agencies are legally required to meet today is built on a standard from 2008 — the same era as the original iPhone. Agencies should treat WCAG 2.0 AA as the floor, not the ceiling.

Legal Requirement

WCAG 2.0 Level AA (published 2008)

Referenced by the Section 508 ICT Standards refresh (effective 2018). Agencies must meet these 38 success criteria to satisfy their Section 508 obligations for web content.

  • Text alternatives for non-text content
  • Captions and audio descriptions for multimedia
  • Keyboard accessibility and focus management
  • Minimum color contrast (4.5:1 for normal text)
  • No seizure-inducing content
  • Consistent navigation and labeling
  • Error identification and correction

Section 508 web content requirements (E205)

Best Practices

WCAG 2.1 and 2.2 Level AA (published 2018 and 2023)

These later versions add criteria that reflect how the web actually works today — mobile devices, cognitive accessibility, and modern authentication patterns — and are strongly recommended for any agency aiming to serve all Americans. The US Federal Government is one of the last to use the legacy WCAG 2.0 AA Standard, as it was developed ~2 decades ago.

  • WCAG 2.1 (2018): 17 new criteria for mobile, low vision, and cognitive accessibility
  • WCAG 2.2 (2023): 9 more criteria for authentication and cognitive tasks, removing the outdated "Parsing" criterion
  • W3C now recommends WCAG 2.2 as the current normative standard for new and updated content

What is new in WCAG 2.2 · WCAG 2.2 specification

The scores and findings in this report are based on automated checks aligned with WCAG 2.x rules surfaced by axe-core. Passing automated checks is necessary but not sufficient: manual testing with assistive technologies is required to verify true conformance and to catch barriers that automation cannot detect.

Take Action#

Today's scan identified 163 accessibility barriers across the most-visited U.S. government websites, affecting an estimated 490,000 Americans with disabilities. Here is how you can help.

  • Read the federal accessibility report. The 2025 Governmentwide Section 508 Assessment from the GSA Administrator details the state of federal accessibility compliance and the steps agencies are taking to improve.
  • Submit URLs you care about. Want specific government pages included in future scans? Submit them to the Open Scans project to help broaden coverage.
  • Test with free tools. Do your own automated and manual accessibility testing with Accessibility Insights and Google Lighthouse. Automated tools catch a significant portion of WCAG failures quickly; manual review with assistive technologies catches the rest.
  • Adopt and update the U.S. Web Design System (USWDS). The USWDS is already a strong foundation for accessible, consistent federal websites, but many agencies have not adopted it or are running outdated versions. Encourage your agency to adopt or upgrade so every American benefits from its accessibility work.
  • Hire people with disabilities. People with disabilities are the ultimate experts on digital barriers. Bringing them into design, engineering, and testing teams — not just as consultants but as full-time employees — leads to more accessible products and services for everyone.

Accessibility Findings

https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 94

Axe findings: 2

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) HHS-1f7ad34a

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~42KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~42,470 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 1,769,582 affected page loads) ~76KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~76,092 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 1,769,582 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-f27034fc

Element path: div.kFwPee > div.qwtKSe > div.cqQpAf > a

Snippet:

<a href="#" jsaction="click:SPLJyd">

Label: here

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.89:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #2f4a8b, surrounding text: #212121)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) HHS-ebb2bc0f

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~38KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~38,931 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 1,769,582 affected page loads) ~102KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Element 1 HHS-5d46b830

Element path: div.row > div.col-lg-3 > p > a.text-white

Snippet:

  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 23.8px instead of at least 24px.

Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main) HHS-98ee53f0

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Accessibility Findings

https://fda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 2

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) HHS-ecc5de53

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4,136 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 413,561 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) HHS-4d2e97e5

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Document doesn't have a `<title>` element (rule: document-title) HHS-804d841a

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt) HHS-bb626cd8

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty

  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) HHS-16f544ab

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

`[role]`s are not contained by their required parent element (rule: aria-required-parent) HHS-f9019603

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) HHS-756f8154

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,224 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 222,371 affected page loads) ~667People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element 1 HHS-7b8cf95b

Element path: div.field__item > div.paragraph > div.ds-u-lg-display--none > button#trigger-tabs-1336969868

Snippet:

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element path: body#type-d > header.ncbi-header > a.usa-skipnav

Snippet:

<a class="usa-skipnav" href="#mainCont">

Label: Skip to main page content

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • No skip link target
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) HHS-b60ad374

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~1,541 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 70,041 affected page loads) ~4KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~4,062 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 70,041 affected page loads)

Affected elements (4):

Element 1 HHS-ce710e53

Element path: div#gn_search > ul > li > a

Snippet:

<a href="Blast.cgi?PAGE_TYPE=BlastSearch&amp;BLAST_SPEC=OGP__9606__9558&amp;LINK_LOC=blasth…">

Label: Human

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (46.7px by 17px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 23px instead of at least 24px.

Accessibility Findings

https://nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 1

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) HHS-3c20b2e6

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~941People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~941 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 39,197 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~1,685 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 39,197 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-67550629

Element path: div.usa-identifier__container > div.usa-identifier__identity > p.usa-identifier__identity-disclaimer > a.ext

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.hhs.gov/" class="ext" data-extlink="" target="_blank" rel="noopener nofollow noreferrer">

Label: Department of Health and Human Services

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.31:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #dfe1e2, surrounding text: #ffffff)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) HHS-08dfb35c

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport) HHS-d8012402

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~188People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~188 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 7,813 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-f7deea39

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1,maximum-scale=1,minimum-scale=1,user-sc…">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Accessibility Findings

https://register.clinicaltrials.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 76

Axe findings: 3

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) HHS-f25f070c

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Estimated ~75 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 7,470 affected page loads) ~22People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~22 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 7,470 affected page loads) ~351People with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~351 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 7,470 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-0cd1fee1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html>

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Form elements do not have associated labels (rule: label) HHS-fb4c12ea

Labels ensure that form controls are announced properly by assistive technologies, like screen readers. Learn more about form element labels.

Disabilities affected: ~75People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~75 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 7,470 affected page loads) ~179People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Affected elements (3):

Element 1 HHS-e2825698

Element path: tbody > tr > td > input

Snippet:

<input type="TEXT" name="Organization" value="" maxlength="60" size="30">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element has no placeholder attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element 2 HHS-e2825698

Element path: tbody > tr > td > input

Snippet:

<input type="TEXT" name="UserName" value="" maxlength="40" size="30">

  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element has no placeholder attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element 3 HHS-e2825698

Element path: tbody > tr > td > input

Snippet:

<input type="PASSWORD" name="Password" value="" autocomplete="off" maxlength="128" size="30">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element has no placeholder attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) HHS-3ade9b96

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Element 1 HHS-5ddf9415

Snippet:

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Fix any of the following:

  • Fix any of the following:

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) HHS-2b55cb65

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://support.nlm.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 88

Axe findings: 4

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) HHS-be8eb6e7

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~56People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~56 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 5,643 affected page loads) ~17People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content.   • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>

  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Form elements do not have associated labels (rule: label) HHS-8021544f

Labels ensure that form controls are announced properly by assistive technologies, like screen readers. Learn more about form element labels.

Disabilities affected: ~55People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~55 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 5,540 affected page loads) ~133People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Affected elements (2):

Element 1 HHS-15ba1d86

Element path: td.disclaimer > div#P101_AGREE > div.apex-item-option > input#P101_AGREE_0

Snippet:

<input type="checkbox" id="P101_AGREE_0" name="P101_AGREE" data-display="" value="Y" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Form element has explicit <label> that is hidden
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element has no placeholder attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element 2 HHS-13b027a6

Element path: tbody > tr > td > input#P_HP_NAME

Snippet:

<input type="text" id="P_HP_NAME" name="P_HP_NAME" class="text_field apex-item-text apex-hp" style="position: absolute !important; left: -99999px !important;" value="" size="30" data-enter-submit="false" data-is-page-item-type="true" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element has no placeholder attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) HHS-2f59774b

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr) HHS-717c2546

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

https://docline.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 1

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) HHS-5f7f9feb

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~82People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~82 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 3,397 affected page loads) ~146People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Affected elements (2):

Element 1 HHS-0391afcb

Element path: div.card-body > div > p > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.nlm.nih.gov/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">

Label: National Library of Medicine's

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 2.43:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0063a4, surrounding text: #212529)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Snippet:

  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) HHS-f857cf60

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Estimated ~58 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 2,628 affected page loads) ~152People with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~152 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 2,628 affected page loads)

Affected elements (13):

Element 1 HHS-fd22fd04

Element path: div.meta-wrapper > ul.post-tags > li > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://ncbiinsights.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/tag/comparative-genomics-resource-cg…" rel="tag">

Label: COMPARATIVE GENOMICS RESOURCE (CGR)

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (280.4px by 16.1px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 19px instead of at least 24px.

https://id.nlm.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 76

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) HHS-5efd01ec

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~52People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~52 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 2,153 affected page loads) ~93People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~93 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 2,153 affected page loads)

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) HHS-062f9725

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) HHS-04e4a51c

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~52People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~52 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 2,153 affected page loads) ~93People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Snippet:

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list) HHS-c6b07fb6

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

Disabilities affected: ~22People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~22 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 2,153 affected page loads) ~6People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~6 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 2,153 affected page loads)

Element 1 HHS-30695a73

Element path: div.menu-and-title > div.row > div.col-md-12 > ul.nav

Snippet:

<ul class="nav nav-pills pull-left">

Label: Home SPARQL Query Editor Lookup Documentation Sample Queries Download API MeSH …

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • List element has direct children that are not allowed: [role=presentation]
List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem) HHS-47b8a646

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) HHS-58d3496c

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Element 1 HHS-5a91f6c5

Element path: header > div.header > div.container > a.header-link

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.fda.gov/" class="header-link">

Label: FDA Logo

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

`[accesskey]` values are not unique (rule: accesskeys) HHS-bc33f52e

Access keys let users quickly focus a part of the page. For proper navigation, each access key must be unique. Learn more about access keys.

Affected elements (2):

Element 1 HHS-9992e3d4

Element path: body > a#nws_header_accesskey_1

Snippet:

<a id="nws_header_accesskey_1" href="https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov" class="usa-sr-only" accesskey="1" tabindex="-1">

Label: NCBI Homepage

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document has multiple elements with the same accesskey

Element 2 HHS-52931a33

Element path: body > a#nws_header_accesskey_3

Snippet:

<a id="nws_header_accesskey_3" href="#maincontent" class="usa-sr-only" accesskey="3" tabindex="-1">

Label: Main Content

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document has multiple elements with the same accesskey

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) HHS-bb92d27d

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block) HHS-4f6a937a

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Label: archival collection

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name) HHS-79dd38c5

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

<button class="menu-toggle" aria-controls="primary-menu" aria-expanded="false">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list) HHS-8f89cabc

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

Disabilities affected: ~9People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~9 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 899 affected page loads) ~3People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~3 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 899 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1 HHS-a4d3a4cc

Element path: div#main-nav > div#nav-menu > div#right-nav > ul.right-links

Snippet:

<ul class="right-links">

Label: Comparison Tool Browse Code Systems Help

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • List element has direct children that are not allowed: [role=button]
Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main) HHS-aaf5ae81

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~9People who are blind or have no functional vision. Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) HHS-850154e4

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast) HHS-d4fcb524

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~18People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~18 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 738 affected page loads) ~32People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) HHS-31a9a9b8

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Accessibility Findings

https://labels.fda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 65

Axe findings: 6

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) HHS-2e5ce04d

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~4People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 414 affected page loads) ~1People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 414 affected page loads) ~19People with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~19 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 414 affected page loads)

Element 1 HHS-699264ce

Snippet:

<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" xmlns:addthis="http://www.addthis.com/help/api-spec">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Form elements do not have associated labels (rule: label) HHS-00bfc9dd

Labels ensure that form controls are announced properly by assistive technologies, like screen readers. Learn more about form element labels.

Disabilities affected: ~4People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 414 affected page loads) ~10People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name) HHS-ae586f1f

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~4People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 414 affected page loads) ~1People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Snippet:

<a href="http://www.fda.gov/Drugs/InformationOnDrugs/ucm142438.htm">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) HHS-9ad6d2f4

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

`[accesskey]` values are not unique (rule: accesskeys) HHS-13650a1a

Access keys let users quickly focus a part of the page. For proper navigation, each access key must be unique. Learn more about access keys.

Accessibility Findings

https://contact.fda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 94

Axe findings: 2

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name) HHS-bc890b7a

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~3People who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 346 affected page loads) ~1People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 346 affected page loads) ~8People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~8 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 346 affected page loads)

Element 1 HHS-b0fb7fa2

Element path: div.region > aside.lcds-card > ul.lcds-description-list > li.lcds-description-list__item

Snippet:

<li role="menuitem" class="lcds-description-list__item">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex) HHS-c6fae3c6

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order) HHS-2a5ad778

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Accessibility Findings

https://gudid.fda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 87

Axe findings: 2

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) HHS-4382f890

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://meshb-prev.nlm.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 88

Axe findings: 3

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr) HHS-751c8b36

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Elements use prohibited ARIA attributes (rule: aria-prohibited-attr) HHS-a1d70b74

Using ARIA attributes in roles where they are prohibited can mean that important information is not communicated to users of assistive technologies. Learn more about prohibited ARIA roles.

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) HHS-a1de0bfc

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) HHS-81202128

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~3People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~3 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 136 affected page loads) ~8People with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~8 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 136 affected page loads)

Affected elements (55):

Element 1 HHS-e7f2ae1e

Element path: div.acim_container > div.acim_links_container > form > button.link

Snippet:

<button type="submit" class="link">

Label: Source code

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (78.6px by 16px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 16px instead of at least 24px.

ARIA input fields do not have accessible names (rule: aria-input-field-name) HHS-da0a753b

When an input field doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn more about input field labels.

Accessibility Findings

https://prstest.clinicaltrials.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 76

Axe findings: 3

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang) HHS-6acdf9d9

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty

  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Accessibility Findings

https://nichd.nih.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 1

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size) HHS-79b1f9d4

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~1People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~1 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 54 affected page loads) ~3People with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~3 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 54 affected page loads)
